title: Case Studies
text: Population ecologists often fit multiple models count data, representing different biological hypotheses growth dynamics. key question whether models distinguishable given data.

title: Case Studies
text: compare three classic growth models: Exponential growth: Unlimited growth constant rate Logistic growth: Growth limited carrying capacity (symmetric S-curve) Gompertz growth: Growth limited carrying capacity (asymmetric S-curve)
code: # Load the example dataset data(ecological_models)  # The data: 50 population counts over time y <- ecological_models$y plot(y, type = "b", xlab = "Time", ylab = "Population count",      main = "Simulated population data")

title: Case Studies
code: # The three competing models models <- ecological_models$models  # View their specifications print(models$exponential) #> <model_spec> Exponential growth  #> Parameters: lambda, r (2) #> Bounds: #>   lambda in (0.1, 200) #>   r in (-2, 2) print(models$logistic) #> <model_spec> Logistic growth  #> Parameters: K, r (2) #> Bounds: #>   K in (1, 200) #>   r in (-2, 2) print(models$gompertz) #> <model_spec> Gompertz growth  #> Parameters: K, r (2) #> Bounds: #>   K in (1, 200) #>   r in (-2, 2)

title: Case Studies
text: models observationally equivalent?
code: # Find equivalence classes among the models classes <- equivalence_classes(models, y, n_points = 25,                                tol = 1e-4, progress = FALSE) print(classes) #> <equiv_classes> #> 3 models -> 3 equivalence classes #>  #> Class 1: exponential #> Class 2: logistic #> Class 3: gompertz

title: Comparing Models
text: vignette covers workflows comparing statistical models determine whether observationally equivalent. Two models equivalent can produce exactly likelihood dataset.

title: Comparing Models
text: First, define two models want compare:
code: # Exponential distribution exp_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, lambda) sum(dexp(y, rate = lambda, log = TRUE)),   par_names = "lambda",   par_bounds = list(lambda = c(1e-6, 100)),   name = "Exponential" )  # Gamma with shape = 1 (mathematically equivalent to exponential) gamma1_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, rate) {     sum(dgamma(y, shape = 1, rate = rate, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = "rate",   par_bounds = list(rate = c(1e-6, 100)),   name = "Gamma(shape=1)" )  # Create the pair pair <- equivalence_pair(exp_spec, gamma1_spec) print(pair) #> <equivalence_pair> Exponential vs Gamma(shape=1)  #> Model A: Exponential (1 parameter) #> Model B: Gamma(shape=1) (1 parameter)

title: Comparing Models
text: Use compare_surfaces() test equivalence:
code: # Generate test data set.seed(42) y <- rexp(100, rate = 2)  # Compare (disable progress bar for vignette) result <- compare_surfaces(pair, y, n_points = 30, progress = FALSE) print(result) #> <surface_comparison> #> Conclusion: Models are NOT EQUIVALENT #> Points tested: 60 #> Max discrepancy: 2.80e-05 #> Tolerance: 1.00e-06 #> Method: grid

title: Comparing Models
text: key outputs : equivalent: Overall conclusion (TRUE/FALSE) max_discrepancy: Largest difference log-likelihood found ab_discrepancy: Max discrepancy mapping → B ba_discrepancy: Max discrepancy mapping B →

title: Comparing Models
text: grid method samples parameter space uniformly checks point can matched: use: General-purpose method, good cases.
code: result_grid <- compare_surfaces(pair, y, n_points = 30,                                 method = "grid", progress = FALSE) result_grid$max_discrepancy #> [1] 2.785233e-05

title: Comparing Models
text: optimization method actively searches counterexamples: use: expect non-equivalence want find clear counterexample quickly.
code: result_optim <- compare_surfaces(pair, y, n_points = 30,                                  method = "optimization", progress = FALSE) result_optim$max_discrepancy #> [1] 3.290648e-05

title: Comparing Models
text: tol parameter determines close likelihoods must : Guidelines: 1e-6 (default): Standard numerical precision 1e-8: strict, may flag numerical artifacts 1e-4: permissive, allows minor numerical differences
code: # Strict tolerance strict <- compare_surfaces(pair, y, n_points = 30, tol = 1e-8, progress = FALSE)  # Relaxed tolerance relaxed <- compare_surfaces(pair, y, n_points = 30, tol = 1e-4, progress = FALSE)  cat("Strict (tol=1e-8):", strict$equivalent, "max discrepancy:", strict$max_discrepancy, "\n") #> Strict (tol=1e-8): FALSE max discrepancy: 3.032093e-05 cat("Relaxed (tol=1e-4):", relaxed$equivalent, "max discrepancy:", relaxed$max_discrepancy, "\n") #> Relaxed (tol=1e-4): TRUE max discrepancy: 1.888234e-05

title: Comparing Models
text: models differ, comparison reveals discrepancy:
code: # Gamma with shape = 2 (NOT equivalent to exponential) gamma2_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, rate) {     sum(dgamma(y, shape = 2, rate = rate, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = "rate",   par_bounds = list(rate = c(1e-6, 100)),   name = "Gamma(shape=2)" )  pair_diff <- equivalence_pair(exp_spec, gamma2_spec) result_diff <- compare_surfaces(pair_diff, y, n_points = 30, progress = FALSE) print(result_diff) #> <surface_comparison> #> Conclusion: Models are NOT EQUIVALENT #> Points tested: 60 #> Max discrepancy: 2.71e+02 #> Tolerance: 1.00e-06 #> Method: grid

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: parameter identifiable value can uniquely determined data. parameters non-identifiable, likelihood function flat direction, meaning infinitely many parameter values produce likelihood.

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: parameters identified regardless sample size:
code: # Only a + b is identifiable, not a and b separately nonid_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, a, b) {     sum(dnorm(y, mean = a + b, sd = 1, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = c("a", "b"),   par_bounds = list(a = c(-10, 10), b = c(-10, 10)),   name = "Non-identifiable sum" )

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: Parameters may theoretically identifiable poorly determined practice:
code: # With limited data, variance-covariance can be hard to estimate bivariate_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, mu1, mu2, sigma1, sigma2, rho) {     # Simplified bivariate normal (ignoring rho for this example)     ll <- dnorm(y[, 1], mu1, sigma1, log = TRUE) +           dnorm(y[, 2], mu2, sigma2, log = TRUE)     sum(ll)   },   par_names = c("mu1", "mu2", "sigma1", "sigma2", "rho"),   par_bounds = list(     mu1 = c(-10, 10), mu2 = c(-10, 10),     sigma1 = c(0.01, 10), sigma2 = c(0.01, 10),     rho = c(-0.99, 0.99)   ),   name = "Bivariate normal" )

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: Use identifiability_check() comprehensive assessment:
code: set.seed(42) y <- rnorm(100, mean = 5, sd = 1)  # Check the non-identifiable model result <- identifiability_check(nonid_spec, y, par = c(a = 2, b = 3)) print(result) #> <identifiability_result> #> Overall: Model has NON-IDENTIFIABLE parameters #>  #> Parameter status: #>   a: NON-IDENTIFIABLE #>   b: NON-IDENTIFIABLE #>  #> Non-identified directions: #>   -0.71*a + 0.71*b #>  #> Identified functions: #>   0.71*a + 0.71*b #>  #> Condition number: 3.1e+12 #> Rank: 1 / 2

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: result shows: Parameter classifications: parameter classified identified, weakly identified, non-identifiable Condition number: High values (> 10^6) suggest ill-conditioning Rank deficiency: Indicates many parameters non-identifiable

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
code: # Define your model model <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, alpha, beta, gamma) {     mu <- alpha + beta * (1 - exp(-gamma * seq_along(y)))     sum(dnorm(y, mean = mu, sd = 1, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = c("alpha", "beta", "gamma"),   par_bounds = list(     alpha = c(-10, 10),     beta = c(0.01, 10),     gamma = c(0.01, 5)   ),   name = "Growth model" )  # Generate test data set.seed(123) n <- 50 true_par <- c(alpha = 1, beta = 5, gamma = 0.5) mu_true <- true_par["alpha"] + true_par["beta"] * (1 - exp(-true_par["gamma"] * (1:n))) y_growth <- rnorm(n, mean = mu_true, sd = 1)

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
code: # Check identifiability at estimated values check <- identifiability_check(model, y_growth, par = true_par) print(check) #> <identifiability_result> #> Overall: Model has POORLY IDENTIFIED parameters #>  #> Parameter status: #>   alpha: NON-IDENTIFIABLE #>   beta: NON-IDENTIFIABLE #>   gamma: identified #>  #> Non-identified directions: #>   0.71*alpha -0.71*beta #>  #> Condition number: 1.4e+03 #> Rank: 3 / 3

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
code: info <- fisher_information(model, y_growth, par = true_par) cat("Condition number:", info_condition(info), "\n") #> Condition number: 1402.097 cat("Eigenvalues:", round(info_eigenvalues(info), 4), "\n") #> Eigenvalues: 112.4737 60.2475 0.0802

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: parameters appear weakly identifiable, profile likelihood provides deeper insight:
code: # Profile each parameter for (p in c("alpha", "beta", "gamma")) {   profile <- profile_likelihood(model, y_growth, par = true_par,                                 which_par = p, n_points = 30)   plot_profile_likelihood(profile) }

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: Transform identifiable parameters:
code: # Instead of estimating a and b separately, # estimate their sum (which IS identifiable) reparam_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, sum_ab) {     sum(dnorm(y, mean = sum_ab, sd = 1, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = "sum_ab",   par_bounds = list(sum_ab = c(-20, 20)),   name = "Reparameterized" )  check_reparam <- identifiability_check(reparam_spec, y, par = c(sum_ab = 5)) print(check_reparam) #> <identifiability_result> #> Overall: Model appears WELL-IDENTIFIED #>  #> Parameter status: #>   sum_ab: identified #>  #> Condition number: 1 #> Rank: 1 / 1

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: Fix non-identifiable parameters interpretable values:
code: # Fix b = 0 and only estimate a fixed_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, a) {     sum(dnorm(y, mean = a + 0, sd = 1, log = TRUE))  # b fixed at 0   },   par_names = "a",   name = "Fixed b" )

title: Identifiability Diagnostics
text: Impose constraints based domain knowledge:
code: # Constrain a > b (not directly enforceable, but useful conceptually) # Often implemented via transformation constrained_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, a, diff) {     b <- a - exp(diff)  # b = a - exp(diff), so b < a always     sum(dnorm(y, mean = a + b, sd = 1, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = c("a", "diff"),   name = "Constrained" )

code: # Exponential model exp_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, lambda) sum(dexp(y, rate = lambda, log = TRUE)),   par_names = "lambda",   par_bounds = list(lambda = c(1e-6, Inf)),   name = "Exponential" )  # Gamma model with shape = 1 (equivalent to exponential) gamma1_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, rate) {     sum(dgamma(y, shape = 1, rate = rate, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = "rate",   par_bounds = list(rate = c(1e-6, Inf)),   name = "Gamma(1)" )  # Gamma model with shape = 2 (not equivalent) gamma2_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, rate) {     sum(dgamma(y, shape = 2, rate = rate, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = "rate",   par_bounds = list(rate = c(1e-6, Inf)),   name = "Gamma(2)" )

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: model_spec fundamental building block degen. encapsulates statistical model log-likelihood function, allowing package perform equivalence testing identifiability analysis.

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: model_spec requires three key components: loglik_fn: function computing log-likelihood par_names: Names model parameters par_bounds: (Optional) Bounds parameter values
code: # A simple normal model norm_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, mu, sigma) {     sum(dnorm(y, mean = mu, sd = sigma, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = c("mu", "sigma"),   par_bounds = list(mu = c(-Inf, Inf), sigma = c(1e-6, Inf)),   name = "Normal" )  print(norm_spec) #> <model_spec> Normal  #> Parameters: mu, sigma (2) #> Bounds: #>   sigma in (1e-06, Inf)

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: log-likelihood function must y first argument (data), followed named parameters:
code: # Correct: y comes first, then named parameters good_fn <- function(y, lambda) {   sum(dexp(y, rate = lambda, log = TRUE)) }  # The parameter names must match those declared in par_names exp_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = good_fn,   par_names = "lambda",   par_bounds = list(lambda = c(1e-6, Inf)),   name = "Exponential" )

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: function must return single numeric value (log-likelihood):
code: # Correct: returns sum (a scalar) correct_ll <- function(y, mu) {   sum(dnorm(y, mean = mu, sd = 1, log = TRUE)) }  # Wrong: returns a vector wrong_ll <- function(y, mu) {   dnorm(y, mean = mu, sd = 1, log = TRUE)  # Missing sum()! }

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: Write defensive code handles problematic parameter values:
code: # A robust mixture model likelihood mixture_ll <- function(y, mu1, mu2, sigma, pi) {   # Guard against invalid parameters   if (pi <= 0 || pi >= 1) return(-Inf)   if (sigma <= 0) return(-Inf)    # Compute likelihood with numerical stability   ll <- log(pi * dnorm(y, mu1, sigma) + (1 - pi) * dnorm(y, mu2, sigma))   sum(ll) }

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: common distributions, degen provides helper functions create properly-specified models: Available helpers include: model_spec_normal() - Normal distribution model_spec_exponential() - Exponential distribution model_spec_gamma() - Gamma distribution model_spec_poisson() - Poisson distribution model_spec_binomial() - Binomial distribution model_spec_beta() - Beta distribution model_spec_lognormal() - Log-normal distribution model_spec_weibull() - Weibull distribution model_spec_negbinom() - Negative binomial distribution model_spec_uniform() - Uniform distribution
code: # Instead of writing the likelihood manually... norm_manual <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, mu, sigma) sum(dnorm(y, mu, sigma, log = TRUE)),   par_names = c("mu", "sigma"),   par_bounds = list(mu = c(-Inf, Inf), sigma = c(1e-6, Inf)),   name = "Normal" )  # ...use the helper norm_helper <- model_spec_normal()  # Both work the same way y <- rnorm(100, mean = 5, sd = 2) loglik(norm_manual, y, c(mu = 5, sigma = 2)) #> [1] -216.127 loglik(norm_helper, y, c(mu = 5, sigma = 2)) #> [1] -216.127

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: Bounds constrain package searches equivalent parameters.

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: Numerical stability: Prevent evaluation invalid values Efficiency: Focus search meaningful regions Physical constraints: Enforce domain knowledge (e.g., rates must positive)
code: # A gamma model with proper bounds gamma_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, shape, rate) {     sum(dgamma(y, shape = shape, rate = rate, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = c("shape", "rate"),   par_bounds = list(     shape = c(1e-6, 100),   # Shape must be positive     rate = c(1e-6, 100)      # Rate must be positive   ),   name = "Gamma" )

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: don’t specify bounds, degen uses c(-Inf, Inf):
code: # No bounds specified - defaults to unbounded unbounded_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, theta) sum(dnorm(y, theta, 1, log = TRUE)),   par_names = "theta",   name = "Location model" )  par_bounds(unbounded_spec) #> $theta #> [1] -Inf  Inf

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: equivalence testing, degen proposes “effective bounds” based data focus search:
code: y <- rexp(100, rate = 2) spec <- model_spec_exponential()  # Internal function proposes data-driven bounds # degen:::propose_bounds(spec, y)

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: Use validate_loglik() test likelihood function:
code: spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, lambda) sum(dexp(y, rate = lambda, log = TRUE)),   par_names = "lambda",   par_bounds = list(lambda = c(1e-6, 100)),   name = "Exponential" )  y <- rexp(50, rate = 2) validate_loglik(spec, y, n_test = 10) #> Validating log-likelihood function... #>  #> 1. Checking return type... OK #> 2. Checking for NA/NaN values... OK #> 3. Checking for +Inf values... OK #> 4. Checking gradient computation... OK #> 5. Checking likelihood ordering... OK #>  #> All checks passed.

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: comprehensive checking, use diagnose_model():
code: # diagnose_model() runs multiple checks diag <- diagnose_model(spec, y, par = c(lambda = 2)) print(diag)

title: Writing Model Specifications
code: # This will fail - 'rate' in function but 'lambda' in par_names model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, rate) sum(dexp(y, rate = rate, log = TRUE)),   par_names = "lambda"  # Mismatch! )

title: Writing Model Specifications
code: # Bad: can return NaN for negative sigma bad_ll <- function(y, mu, sigma) {   sum(dnorm(y, mu, sigma, log = TRUE))  # NaN if sigma < 0 }  # Better: return -Inf for invalid regions better_ll <- function(y, mu, sigma) {   if (sigma <= 0) return(-Inf)   sum(dnorm(y, mu, sigma, log = TRUE)) }

title: Writing Model Specifications
code: # Wrong: trying to use a vector parameter bad_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, beta) {     # beta is expected to be length 2     sum(dnorm(y, mean = beta[1], sd = beta[2], log = TRUE))   },   par_names = "beta"  # Only one name but expects vector! )  # Correct: use separate named parameters good_spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, beta1, beta2) {     sum(dnorm(y, mean = beta1, sd = beta2, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = c("beta1", "beta2") )

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: Attach simulation function bootstrap power analysis:
code: spec <- model_spec_normal() spec <- add_simulator(spec, function(n, mu, sigma) rnorm(n, mu, sigma))  # Now you can simulate data can_simulate(spec) #> [1] TRUE simulated_y <- simulate(spec, n = 100, par = c(mu = 5, sigma = 2)) head(simulated_y) #> [1] 6.350489 7.306752 1.626991 3.194370 7.635267 7.200379

title: Writing Model Specifications
text: Create model_spec existing fitted model:
code: # Fit a linear model data <- data.frame(x = 1:50, y = 2 + 3 * (1:50) + rnorm(50)) fit <- lm(y ~ x, data = data)  # Extract as model_spec spec <- model_spec_from_fit(fit) print(spec) #> <model_spec> LM: y ~ x  #> Parameters: X_Intercept_, x, sigma (3) #> Bounds: #>   sigma in (1e-10, Inf)

title: Compute Fisher information matrix — fisher_information
text: spec model_spec object y Numeric vector observed data par Named numeric vector parameter values evaluate type Type information: "observed" (default) uses negative Hessian data; "expected" uses outer product gradients (OPG) estimator computed per-observation score contributions method Computation method: "hessian" (default) uses numerical differentiation cl Optional parallel cluster setup_cluster(). used type = "expected" parallelize per-observation score computation.

title: Compute Fisher information matrix — fisher_information
text: S3 object class fisher_info containing: matrix Fisher information matrix eigenvalues Eigenvalues (sorted decreasing) eigenvectors Corresponding eigenvectors condition Condition number rank Numerical rank par Parameter values used par_names Parameter names type Type information computed

title: Compute Fisher information matrix — fisher_information
text: Fisher information matrix characterizes curvature log-likelihood surface. Key properties: Positive definite: parameters locally identifiable Singular (rank deficient): parameters identifiable Near-singular (high condition number): parameters weakly identified Two types information available: Observed information (type = "observed"): negative Hessian log-likelihood. default common choice. Expected information (type = "expected"): Estimated using outer product gradients (OPG), scores summed observations. score gradient log-likelihood observation. regularity conditions, converges limit observed information may differ finite samples. Requires log-likelihood additive observations.

title: Compute Fisher information matrix — fisher_information
code: spec <- model_spec(   loglik_fn = function(y, mu, sigma) {     sum(dnorm(y, mean = mu, sd = sigma, log = TRUE))   },   par_names = c("mu", "sigma"),   par_bounds = list(sigma = c(1e-6, Inf)) )  set.seed(123) y <- rnorm(100, mean = 5, sd = 2)  # Observed information (negative Hessian) info_obs <- fisher_information(spec, y, par = c(mu = 5, sigma = 2)) print(info_obs)  # Expected information (OPG estimator) info_exp <- fisher_information(spec, y, par = c(mu = 5, sigma = 2),                                type = "expected") print(info_exp)
